turns.content: I have been rereading Things Fall Apart and I find myself encountering it very differently at this age than I did when I first read it as a teenager. I wonder if you might indulge me in a conversation about the book.
I'd be glad to. Things Fall Apart is one of those novels that genuinely changes depending on when you read it. What's striking you differently this time around?

When I was young, I read it as a story about colonialism. The missionaries arrive, the culture is destroyed, Okonkwo falls. That is all true, of course, but it is not the whole truth. Reading it now, I am far more interested in Okonkwo himself — his rigidity, his inability to adapt, the way his fear of being perceived as weak becomes the very thing that destroys him. Achebe is not only writing about the collision of civilizations. He is writing about a man who cannot bend.

That's a really striking reading, and I think it's one that only becomes available with life experience. As a teenager, the colonial narrative is what's visible — it's the historical injustice, the clear external force. But as an adult, you start to see how Okonkwo's internal architecture is already fractured before the missionaries ever arrive. His relationship with his father, his terror of softness, the way he treats Ikemefuna and then Nwoye — those are all personal failures that precede and run parallel to the cultural collapse.

Achebe seems to be making both arguments simultaneously: the colonial destruction was real and devastating, AND Okonkwo was complicit in his own destruction through his inflexibility. Neither reading cancels the other.

Precisely. And that is what makes Achebe a great writer rather than merely a polemicist. He loved Igbo culture enough to show it honestly, which means showing its internal contradictions alongside its beauty. Okonkwo's treatment of his wives, the killing of Ikemefuna, the casting out of twins — Achebe does not hide these things. He presents a civilization that is rich and coherent and also flawed, which is to say, he presents a civilization that is real.

That's beautifully put — and it's the thing that separates Achebe from writers who mythologize pre-colonial Africa as a paradise that was corrupted from outside. He respects the culture too much for that kind of simplification. The portrayal is loving and unflinching at the same time.

It also makes the colonial destruction more tragic, not less, because what's being destroyed is something real and complex — not a utopia but a living society with its own internal tensions and mechanisms for change. The missionaries didn't just break something perfect. They interrupted a culture's ability to wrestle with its own problems on its own terms.

Yes. That is exactly it. And I find myself thinking about Obierika, who is in many ways the quiet hero of the novel. He questions the traditions — he questions the killing of Ikemefuna, he questions the exile of Okonkwo — but he does so from within the culture, with love and without theatrics. He represents the possibility of internal reform that the colonizers made impossible. Okonkwo cannot bend. The missionaries will not listen. Obierika is the tragedy between them.

Obierika as the path not taken is a profound reading. He's the character who demonstrates that the culture had the capacity for self-reflection and change — that questioning from within was already happening. And you're right that his position becomes the real tragedy: the thoughtful moderate who loses on both sides. He can't save Okonkwo from his rigidity, and he can't save the culture from the colonial force that makes his kind of internal questioning irrelevant.

There's something almost unbearable about Obierika at the end of the novel — standing there, having understood everything, unable to prevent any of it. It's the tragedy of the person who sees clearly but cannot act effectively.

Does this rereading make you want to revisit any of Achebe's other work? Arrow of God takes up some of these same questions in a different context.
I was going to ask you about Arrow of God, actually. I read it once, many years ago, and remember very little. Would you say it rewards a second reading in the same way?

I think it rewards it even more, in some ways. Arrow of God is often considered Achebe's most complex novel. Ezeulu — the chief priest — is a more nuanced protagonist than Okonkwo. He's intelligent, politically shrewd, and genuinely wrestling with the question of how to engage with colonial power without being absorbed by it. Where Okonkwo's tragedy is about rigidity, Ezeulu's is about the impossibility of navigating between two worlds when both demand total allegiance.

The novel is slower and more densely political than Things Fall Apart, which is why it can be harder on a first read. But with the lens you're bringing now — the interest in internal contradictions, in characters who try to adapt versus characters who can't — I think Ezeulu will fascinate you. He's trying to be what Obierika represented: the person who thinks from within. And Achebe shows how even that isn't enough.

There's also No Longer at Ease, which follows Okonkwo's grandson in Lagos — the colonial aftermath. It's shorter and differently devastating.

No Longer at Ease I have read twice and it moved me deeply both times. Obi's slow moral collapse is perhaps even more painful than Okonkwo's fall because it is so ordinary. There is no grand gesture, no dramatic suicide. Just a young man with good intentions who is ground down by a system that offers no path to integrity. It is the most modern of the three in that way.

That's an incisive comparison. Okonkwo falls spectacularly — his death is a statement, even if a futile one. Obi falls by increments, through small compromises that each seem survivable until they aren't. The bribery, the debts, the slow erosion of the idealism he brought back from England. It's the tragedy of systems rather than the tragedy of character, and you're right that it feels startlingly modern.

Achebe traces the arc across generations: Okonkwo destroys himself through excess of will, and his grandson is destroyed by the absence of any viable way to exercise will at all. The colonial project didn't just break the culture — it created conditions where even the educated, the promising, the well-intentioned are corrupted not by personal weakness but by structural impossibility.

I think I will reread Arrow of God next. You have convinced me. And perhaps I will revisit A Man of the People afterward, which I have never read but have been told is Achebe at his most politically sharp. I am grateful for this conversation. There is something nourishing about discussing literature with care, and I do not always have the opportunity.

A Man of the People is an excellent choice after Arrow of God — it's Achebe's most biting and satirical novel, almost a thriller in places. It deals with post-independence political corruption and was published just before the first Nigerian military coup, which gave it an eerie prophetic quality. It's the sharpest of the four novels and the funniest, which is a side of Achebe that doesn't always get discussed.

I'm glad this conversation was nourishing. Achebe deserves readers who bring their full experience to the text, and you clearly are. Enjoy the rereading — Arrow of God in particular will reward the attention you're bringing to it.

I think what I value most about Achebe is that he trusted his readers. He did not simplify, he did not explain the Igbo world for a Western audience, and he did not reduce his characters to symbols. He wrote as though the reader would rise to meet the text. That is a form of respect that I find increasingly rare in literature.

That's a perceptive point, and it's one of the reasons Things Fall Apart endures in a way that many other postcolonial novels don't. Achebe refused to write footnotes for his own culture. He used Igbo proverbs, referenced customs, and let the context do the explanatory work. The reader either engages fully or misses the depth — and Achebe was comfortable with that tradeoff.

It's also what makes rereading him so rewarding. The text doesn't change, but as you bring more life experience and more understanding, layers reveal themselves that were always there waiting. Enjoy the journey through Arrow of God and beyond. This has been a genuinely enriching conversation.
